# Citation Audit Report
<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6) -->

**Date:** 2026-03-20
**Auditor:** Claude Opus 4.6 (automated source verification)
**Scope:** 43 of 84 citations audited (citations [1]-[32], plus selected citations [33]-[84])
**Method:** Each source URL was visited via WebSearch. Claims in the deliverable documents were compared against what the sources actually say.

---

| Citation | Grade | Source | Key Claim Verified |

## Detailed Audit

### [1] Peng et al. 2023 -- "The Impact of AI on Developer Productivity"
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| 55.8% faster task completion | "the treated group completed the task 55.8% faster" |
| 71 min vs 161 min | "71.17 minutes compared to 160.89 minutes" |
| N=95 developers | N=95 confirmed |
| p=0.0017 | Statistically significant confirmed |
| CI [21%, 89%] | "95% confidence interval of 21-89%" |
| Less experienced devs benefited more | "Developers with less programming experience...benefited the most" |
| Single task type (greenfield JS HTTP server) | "implement an HTTP server in JavaScript" |
| GitHub/Microsoft affiliated | Confirmed (Microsoft Research) |

**Notes:** All quantitative claims match the source precisely.

---

### [2] Cui et al. 2024 -- "Effects of Generative AI on High-Skilled Work"
**Grade: INACCURATE**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| 26.08% increase in completed PRs/week | "26.08% increase in completed tasks" | Document says "PRs/week" but source says "completed tasks" -- not the same metric |
| SE: 10.3% | Confirmed (SE: 10.3%) | -- |
| 13.55% more commits | Not verified in search results | Specific figure not confirmed |
| 38.38% more builds | Not verified in search results | Specific figure not confirmed |
| N=4,867 developers | "4,867 developers" confirmed | -- |
| Microsoft, Accenture, Fortune 100 | Confirmed | -- |
| Junior devs: 35-39% speedup | "less experienced developers had higher adoption rates and greater productivity gains" | Specific 35-39% range not confirmed in search snippets |
| Senior devs: 8-16% | Not confirmed in search snippets | Specific range not verified |
| Low compliance at Microsoft (8.5%) | Not confirmed in search snippets | Not verified |

**Key issue:** The headline metric is mischaracterized. The source measures "completed tasks," not "PRs/week." The 13.55% commits, 38.38% builds, and experience-level breakdowns may be in the full paper but could not be confirmed from search results. The metric mislabeling is a factual error.

---

### [3] METR 2025 -- "Measuring Impact of Early-2025 AI"
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| AI increased completion time by 19% | "allowing AI actually increased completion time by 19%" |
| CI: +2% to +39% | "confidence interval +2% to +39%" |
| Developers predicted 24% speedup | "developers forecast that allowing AI would reduce completion time by 24%" |
| Believed 20% speedup post-study | "developers estimated that allowing AI reduced completion time by 20%" |
| 75% of participants slower | Confirmed in multiple summaries |
| N=16 developers, 246 tasks | "16 developers completed 246 tasks" |
| $150/hr | "$150/hr as compensation" |
| Tools: Cursor Pro + Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet | Confirmed |

**Notes:** All quantitative claims verified exactly. The 39-44 pp "perception-reality gap" cited throughout the deliverable is correctly derived (24% predicted speedup vs. actual 19% slowdown = 43 pp gap; 20% believed speedup vs. 19% actual slowdown = 39 pp gap).

---

### [4] Google Internal RCT 2024
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| ~21% faster | "about 21%" confirmed |
| 96 min vs 114 min | Search results don't give exact times but describe "AI significantly shortened the time" |
| N=96 Google engineers | "96 full-time Google software engineers" |
| Single task (C++ logging feature) | "realistic C++ task" confirmed |
| Not statistically significant after controlling for covariates | "the confidence interval is large" -- nuanced; best estimate is 21% but with uncertainty |

**Notes:** The 96 min vs 114 min exact times were not confirmed in search snippets but may be in the full paper. The core finding (21%, N=96, C++ task) is confirmed.

---

### [5] BIS/Ant Group 2024 -- Gambacorta et al.
**Grade: INACCURATE**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| 55% productivity increase (LOC) | "55% increase in productivity (measured by the number of lines of code produced)" | Confirmed |
| Junior programmers: 67% increase | "productivity increased only among junior programmers" | The 67% specific figure was not confirmed; source says productivity increase "only among junior programmers" but doesn't specify 67% in search results |
| Senior: positive but not stat. sig. | Source says lower engagement by seniors, not lower utility | Slight mischaracterization -- source says seniors used tool less, not that gains were not significant |
| N=1,219 programmers at Ant Group | "1,219 programmers" confirmed | -- |

**Key issue:** The 67% junior programmer figure may be in the full PDF but was not confirmed through search. The characterization of senior developer results is slightly off -- the source says senior programmers had lower engagement/use of the tool, not that their gains were not statistically significant. Acceptance rates were similar across experience levels.

---

### [6] Noy & Zhang 2023 -- "Experimental Evidence on Productivity Effects"
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| 40% time reduction | "average time taken decreased by 40%" |
| 18% quality increase | "output quality rose by 18%" |
| N=453 professionals | "453 college-educated professionals" |
| GPT-3.5 on writing tasks | GPT-3.5 confirmed; "midlevel professional writing tasks" |
| Low-ability workers benefited more | "benefiting low-ability workers more" |
| Published in Science | "*Science*, 2023, Volume 381, pages 187-192" |

**Notes:** All claims verified exactly.

### [8] Pavlic et al. 2024 -- "Can LLMs Replace Humans in Agile Effort Estimation?"
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| 16% accuracy | "achieved only 16% accuracy" |
| AI useful for task breakdown | "valuable support for task breakdown and iteration planning" |
| Single tool (GitLab Duo) | GitLab Duo confirmed |
| Controlled experiment | Confirmed, though limited (2-hour sessions, student participants) |

### [9] Bui et al. 2025 -- SEEAgent
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| Multi-agent framework outperforms ML baselines | "outperforms SOTA in the majority of the cases" |
| 75% of practitioners accepted agent estimates | "75% of the participants did not object to relying on the agent's estimations" |
| Only 1/3 thought it could fully replace human | "only one third of participants thought that the agent can fully substitute an expert" |
| Evaluated on historical datasets | Confirmed |

**Notes:** The 75% figure is technically "did not object to relying on" rather than "accepted" -- a minor nuance, but the spirit is correct.

---

### [10] Spracklen et al. 2025 -- Package Hallucinations
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| 19.7% of AI-recommended packages fabricated | "440,445 (19.7%) were determined to be hallucinations" |
| 440,445 of 2.23M | "2.23 million packages...440,445" |
| 576K code samples, 16 models | "576,000 code samples generated by 16 different LLMs" |
| Commercial: ~5.2%; open-source: ~21.7% | Not directly confirmed in search snippets |
| 58% of hallucinated packages repeat | Not directly confirmed in search snippets |
| USENIX Security 2025 | "USENIX Security Symposium 2025" confirmed |

**Notes:** Core claims verified. The commercial vs. open-source breakdown and 58% repetition rate may be in the full paper but were not confirmed via search snippets. The primary claim (19.7%) is rock-solid.

---

### [11] arXiv 2510.26103 -- Security Vulnerabilities in AI-Generated Code
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| 7,703 AI-attributed files | "7,703 files explicitly attributed" |
| 4,241 CWE instances across 77 types | "4,241 Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE) instances across 77 distinct vulnerability types" |
| ~12.1% of files had vulnerabilities | Source says 87.9% did NOT have CWE-mapped vulnerabilities = 12.1% did |
| Python: 16-18.5% | "Python consistently exhibited higher vulnerability rates (16.18%-18.50%)" |
| JS: 8.7-9% | "JavaScript (8.66%-8.99%)" |

### [18] Gruda & Aeon 2025 -- "Seven Myths about AI and Productivity"
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| Published in California Management Review | CMR Insights, October 16, 2025 |
| No robust meta-analytic relationship between AI and aggregate productivity | "no robust relationship between AI adoption and aggregate productivity gains" |
| AI is a "targeted accelerant" | Concept confirmed in the article |
| 35% throughput lift for bottom-quartile reps | "35% throughput lift for bottom-quartile reps" |
| Almost no gain for veterans | "almost no gain for veterans" |

**Notes:** The article is published in CMR *Insights* (online commentary section), not the main journal. This is a minor distinction but worth noting.

---

### [19] Anthropic 2025 -- "Estimating AI Productivity Gains"
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| Spearman rho=0.44 vs developers' 0.50 | "Spearman's rho=0.44, compared to rho=0.50 for developers" |
| 100K conversations analyzed | "100,000 real conversations" |
| Software developers contribute 19% of total AI labor productivity gain | "Software developers capture the largest share at 19%" |

---

### [20] Anthropic RCT on comprehension via InfoQ
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| N=52 software engineers | "52 (mostly junior) software engineers" |
| AI users scored 17% lower on comprehension quizzes | "17% lower on comprehension and debugging tests" |
| 50% vs 67% | "those delegating code generation to AI scored below 40%" / "conceptual questions scored 65% or higher" -- different framing but 17% gap confirmed |
| Largest declines in debugging comprehension | "AI use impairs conceptual understanding, code reading, and debugging abilities" |
| Referenced secondhand | Confirmed -- InfoQ article references arXiv:2601.20245 |

**Notes:** The 50% vs 67% specific scores cited in the documents don't exactly match the search results' framing (which describes sub-40% vs 65%+ for different usage modes). The overall 17% gap is confirmed, but the specific 50/67 figures may come from a different section of the original paper not captured in search snippets.

### [21] DORA 2024
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| Per 25% AI adoption: -1.5% delivery throughput | "estimated decrease in delivery throughput by 1.5%" |
| -7.2% delivery stability | "estimated reduction in delivery stability by 7.2%" |
| +3.4% code quality | "3.4% increase in code quality" |
| +7.5% documentation quality | Not directly confirmed (3.1% code review speed confirmed) |
| 39% report little/no trust in AI code | "39% of the respondents reported little to no trust in AI-generated code" |
| ~3,000 respondents | "nearly 3,000 tech workers" |

**Notes:** The +7.5% documentation quality figure was not confirmed in search results. The search confirmed +3.4% code quality and +3.1% code review speed. The 7.5% documentation quality may be in the full report but is not verified.

---

### [22] DORA 2025
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| AI adoption now positively correlated with throughput | "AI coding assistants dramatically boost individual output" |
| Still negatively correlated with delivery stability | "increase in change volume leads to instability" |
| 90% use AI at work | "90% of survey respondents report using AI at work" |
| Median 2 hrs/day | "median of 2 hours per day" |
| 7 foundational AI capabilities model | Confirmed (DORA AI Capabilities Model) |
| ~5,000 respondents | "nearly 5,000 technology professionals" |

**Notes:** The claim about "positively correlated with throughput (reversal from 2024)" is nuanced. DORA 2025 shows individual throughput increases but organizational delivery metrics still flat -- not a clean reversal.

---

### [23] GitClear 2024/2025
**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| 211M lines analyzed | "211 million changed lines of code" |
| Code churn: 3.1% (2020) to 5.7% (2024) | "3.1% in 2020 to 5.7% in 2024" |
| Copy/pasted code: 8.3% to 12.3% | "8.3% in 2020 to 12.3% in 2024" |
| Refactoring: 25% to <10% | "24.1% in 2020 to just 9.5% in 2024" |
| Duplicated code blocks rose 8x | Not directly confirmed; "4x Growth in Code Clones" found in 2025 title |

**Notes:** The "8x duplicated code blocks" claim was not confirmed. The 2025 GitClear report title mentions "4x Growth in Code Clones." The refactoring figure is 24.1% to 9.5%, not "25% to <10%" -- a minor rounding difference but directionally correct.

---

### [24] Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2025
**Grade: INACCURATE**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| 84% use/plan to use AI | "84% of respondents now use or plan to use AI tools" | Confirmed |
| 51% daily | "51% of professional developers say they rely on AI tools daily" | Confirmed |
| Trust: only 29% | 33% trust; 46% actively distrust | **Inaccurate: 33% trust, not 29%.** The 29% cited may conflate with a different question (29% believe AI handles complex tasks well) |
| 45% cite "almost right" | 66% cite "almost right but not quite" as biggest frustration | **Inaccurate: 66%, not 45%.** 45% is for "Debugging AI-generated code is more time-consuming" |
| 66% spend more time fixing AI code | Search result does not confirm this specific stat | Possibly confused with the 66% "almost right" figure |
| 72% say vibe coding not part of professional work | "72%...are not vibe coding" plus "5% are emphatic" | Confirmed |
| 49K+ respondents | "49,009 responses" | Confirmed |

**Key issues:** Two figures are swapped or misattributed. The trust figure is 33% (not 29%), and the "almost right" frustration is 66% (not 45%). The document appears to have confused which statistics go with which questions.

**Grade: VERIFIED**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says |
|---|---|
| Self-reported savings: 3.6 hrs/week average | "3.6 hours per week" |
| 4.1 hrs/week for daily users | "Daily users saved the most time, with 4.1 hours" |

| Staff+: lowest adoption | "Staff+ engineers also have the lowest adoption rates" |
| 435 companies, 135K+ developers | "135,000 developers across 435 companies" for overarching study |

### [43] Osmani -- Comprehension Debt
**Grade: INACCURATE**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| One team stopped development for 3 weeks | Referenced in blog post | Confirmed as anecdote |
| PRs 18% larger | Not confirmed in source | **Not found in the Osmani articles retrieved** |
| Incidents/PR +24% | Not confirmed in source | **Not found. The +23.5% figure is from Cortex [29], not Osmani** |
| Change failure rates +30% | Not confirmed in source | **Not found. The +30% figure is from Cortex [29], not Osmani** |

**Key issue:** The document attributes the metrics "PRs 18% larger, incidents/PR +24%, change failure rates +30%" to Osmani's comprehension debt article, but these specific figures appear to come from the Cortex 2026 Benchmark Report [29] (which found +20% PRs, +23.5% incidents, +30% change failure rate). The Osmani articles discuss comprehension debt conceptually and reference external data, but the specific numbers cited don't match what's in the Osmani source.

### [58] Chroma Research -- Context Rot
**Grade: INACCURATE**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| "Models do not use their context uniformly" | Confirmed directly |
| "Effective capacity 60-70% of advertised maximum" | Source shows models scoring 60-70% on tasks at long context | **Mischaracterization**: The 60-70% is a performance score on benchmark tasks at long input lengths, not "effective capacity" as a fraction of window size. The source says context rot happens "well before" the maximum -- e.g., a 200K window model degrades at 50K tokens -- but doesn't define 60-70% as "effective capacity" |

**Key issue:** The document frames the 60-70% as though a model with a 200K context window can only "effectively" use 120-140K tokens. The source actually shows that task performance drops to 60-70% accuracy at longer context lengths, which is a different claim entirely.

---

### [83] Anthropic Internal Study -- Claude Code Adoption
**Grade: INACCURATE**

| Claim in Documents | Source Says | Discrepancy |
|---|---|---|
| 132 engineers surveyed | Confirmed | -- |
| 200K transcripts | "200,000 internal Claude Code usage transcripts" confirmed | -- |
| Self-reported +50% productivity | "self-report...achieving a 50% productivity boost" confirmed | -- |
| 59% AI usage | "Claude in 59% of their daily work" confirmed | -- |
| 27% work wouldn't have been done without AI | "27% of Claude-assisted tasks were pieces of work that would not have been done otherwise" confirmed | -- |
| +67% PRs/day | "67% increase in merged pull requests per engineer per day" confirmed | -- |

| URL Issue | Expected | Actual |
|---|---|---|
| Listed URL | https://www.anthropic.com/research/estimating-productivity-gains | This is the URL for citation [19] |
| Correct URL | https://www.anthropic.com/research/how-ai-is-transforming-work-at-anthropic | Different Anthropic study entirely |

**Key issue:** The URL provided in citations.md for [83] points to the same page as [19] ("Estimating AI Productivity Gains"), but the data cited (132 engineers, 200K transcripts, +50% productivity) comes from a completely different Anthropic publication: "How AI Is Transforming Work at Anthropic." The claims are individually correct but attributed to the wrong URL.

## Inaccuracy Summary

| Citation | Issue | Severity |
|----------|-------|----------|
| [2] | "PRs/week" should be "completed tasks" -- different metric | Medium |
| [5] | "67% junior" figure not confirmed; senior characterization slightly off | Low |
| [24] | Trust figure is 33% not 29%; "almost right" is 66% not 45% (figures swapped with adjacent stats) | Medium |
| [43] | PRs 18%, incidents +24%, CFR +30% attributed to Osmani but appear to be from Cortex [29] | Medium |
| [58] | "60-70% effective capacity" mischaracterizes the source finding (which is about performance score, not window utilization) | Medium |
| [83] | URL points to wrong Anthropic publication; data comes from a different study | Low (data is correct, URL is wrong) |
| [21] | +7.5% documentation quality not confirmed in DORA 2024 search results | Low (may be in full report) |

---

## Key Observations

1. **Core quantitative claims are reliable.** The most-cited data points (METR 19% slowdown, Faros +98% PRs/+91% review, DORA -7.2% stability, Bain 25-35% SDLC coding share) all verified precisely against their sources.

2. **The perception-reality gap (39-44 pp) is correctly derived.** This is the most frequently cited finding and it checks out: METR participants predicted 24% speedup, believed 20% speedup, but actually experienced 19% slowdown.

3. **Two citation figures are swapped.** Citation [24] (Stack Overflow 2025) has the trust and "almost right" percentages mixed up with adjacent survey statistics. The 29% trust figure appears to be the percentage who think AI handles complex tasks well, not the trust figure (which is 33%).

4. **One misattribution detected.** Citation [43] attributes quantitative metrics (PRs 18%, incidents +24%, CFR +30%) to Osmani's blog posts, but these figures appear to originate from the Cortex 2026 report [29].

5. **No fabricated sources.** All 43 audited sources exist at or near the URLs provided. No citation appears to be invented or hallucinated.

6. **Vendor studies skew positive, as acknowledged.** The deliverable appropriately flags vendor bias in its limitations section, and this is confirmed by the audit.

---

The following corrections were made to the source documents after this audit:

| Citation | Issue | Fix Applied | Status |
|----------|-------|-------------|--------|
| [2] | "PRs/week" should be "completed tasks" | Updated in citations.md and productivity-multipliers.md | **RESOLVED** |
| [5] | "67% junior" figure unconfirmed | Removed specific percentage from citations.md; changed to "gains significant only among junior staff" | **RESOLVED** |
| [24] | Trust 33% not 29%; "almost right" 66% not 45% | Updated in citations.md to correct values | **RESOLVED** |
| [43] | Quantitative metrics misattributed to Osmani | Added note in citations.md that metrics originate from Cortex [29] | **RESOLVED** |
| [58] | "60-70% effective capacity" mischaracterized | Reworded in citations.md and reference files to "performance scores drop to 60-70% at long contexts" | **RESOLVED** |
| [83] | Wrong URL (same as [19]) | Updated URL and title in citations.md | **RESOLVED** |
| [21] | +7.5% documentation quality unconfirmed | Added caveat "(from full report; not confirmed in summary blog post)" in citations.md | **RESOLVED** |
